html:
[810]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 靖亦驰马速征 - Li Jing rides to the capital quickly, reflecting the urgency and importance of his mission.
  • 俄即到京 - Soon he arrived in the capital.
  • 与张氏同往 - Zhang Shifu, the woman, accompanies Li Jing, indicating their close relationship.
  • 至一小版门 - The small wooden gate suggests a humble and unassuming entrance, perhaps reflecting the modest origins of the household.
  • 有应者出 - Someone answers the knock, showing hospitality and readiness to welcome guests.
  • 三郎令候李郎、一娘子久矣 - The Third Brother (likely Li Yuanji) has been waiting for Li Jing and Zhang Shifu, emphasizing the importance of their visit.
  • 延人重门 - The main gate is opened, symbolizing the transition from the outside world to the inner sanctum of the household.
  • 门益壮丽 - The gate is described as magnificent, indicating the wealth and status of the household.
  • 奴婢三十余人 - The presence of more than thirty servants suggests a large and well-staffed household.
  • 罗列庭前 - The servants are arranged in rows, showing order and discipline.
  • 青衣二十人 - Twenty people in green robes, likely attendants or servants, are present.
  • 引靖人东厅 - Led Li Jing to the east hall.
  • 厅之陈设，穷极珍异 - The furnishings of the hall were extremely precious and rare.
  • 巾箱妆奁冠镜首饰之盛 - The abundance of hairpins, makeup boxes, hats, mirrors, and jewelry.
  • 非人间之物 - Not things found in the human world.
  • 巾栉妆饰毕备 - All the hairpins, combs, makeup, and decorations were ready.
  • 请更衣 - Asked to change clothes.
  • 衣又珍奇 - The clothes were also precious and unusual.
  • 甫毕 - Just finished.
  • 传云 - A servant announced.
  • 三郎来 - The Third Brother has arrived.
  • 乃虬髯也 - It was the man with the curling beard.
  • 纱帽紫衫 - Wearing a gauze hat and purple robe.
  • 趋走有龙虎之状 - Moving with the appearance of a dragon or tiger.
  • 相见欢然 - They met happily.
  • 命妻出拜 - Ordered his wife to come out and bow.
  • 亦天人也 - She was also a heavenly being.
  • 遂延中堂 - Then led them to the central hall.
  • 陈设盘筵之盛 - The table was set with abundant dishes.
  • 虽王公亦不侔也 - Even kings and princes could not compare.
  • 四人对坐 - The four of them sat facing each other.
  • 陈馔 - Set out the food.
  • 次出女乐二十人 - Then brought out twenty female musicians.
  • 旅奏于庭 - Played music in the courtyard.
  • 似从天降 - As if they had descended from heaven.
  • 非人间之曲度 - Not the melodies of the human world.
  • 食毕 - After they had finished eating.
  • 行酒 - Passed around the wine.
  • 有苍头自西堂异出二十床 - There were twenty tables brought out by a servant from the west hall.
  • 各覆以锦帕 - Each covered with brocade cloths.
  • 既列 - After they were arranged.
  • 尽去其帕 - They removed all the cloths.
  • 乃文簿钥匙之类 - They turned out to be documents and keys.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 靖亦驰马速征 - Li Jing rides to the capital quickly, reflecting the urgency and importance of his mission.
  • 与张氏同往 - Zhang Shifu, the woman, accompanies Li Jing, indicating their close relationship.
  • 至一小版门 - The small wooden gate suggests a humble and unassuming entrance, perhaps reflecting the modest origins of the household.
  • 有应者出 - Someone answers the knock, showing hospitality and readiness to welcome guests.
  • 三郎令候李郎、一娘子久矣 - The Third Brother (likely Li Yuanji) has been waiting for Li Jing and Zhang Shifu, emphasizing the importance of their visit.
  • 延人重门 - The main gate is opened, symbolizing the transition from the outside world to the inner sanctum of the household.
  • 门益壮丽 - The gate is described as magnificent, indicating the wealth and status of the household.
  • 奴婢三十余人 - The presence of more than thirty servants suggests a large and well-staffed household.
  • 罗列庭前 - The servants are arranged in rows, showing order and discipline.
  • 青衣二十人 - Twenty people in green robes, likely attendants or servants, are present.

[811]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 虬髯舉杯告靖曰 - Direct speech act where Qiu Rán raises his cup and speaks to Jing.
  • 此皆珍寶貨帛之數 - Refers to a list of valuable items, including precious treasures and goods.
  • 吾之所有，悉有充贈 - Indicates a generous offer of all possessions.
  • 何者？ - Introduces an explanation or question, often translated as 'Why?' or 'What is the reason?'
  • 某本欲于此世界求事，当或龙战二三十年，建少功业 - Describes a grand ambition of seeking matters in the world, possibly involving intense competition or struggle, and establishing some achievements.
  • 今既有主，住亦何为？ - Expresses a sense of resignation, questioning the purpose of staying when there is already a master.
  • 太原李氏，真英主也 - Praises the Li family of Taiyuan as a truly outstanding ruler.
  • 三五年内，即当太平 - Predicts that peace will be established within three to five years.
  • 李郎以英特之才，辅清平之主，竭心尽力，必极人臣 - Predicts a successful career for Li Lang, who will reach the highest rank among ministers by assisting a clear and peaceful ruler.
  • 一妹以天人之姿，蕴不世之艺，从夫之贵，荣及轩裳 - Describes the younger sister's noble life, with her heavenly beauty and rare talents, gaining honor and glory through her husband's status.
  • 非一妹不能识李郎，非李郎不能遇一妹 - Emphasizes the mutual recognition between the younger sister and Li Lang.
  • 圣贤起陆之渐，际会如期，虎啸风生，龙腾云合，固非偶然也 - Describes a significant event, with the rise of sages and virtuous men, timely meetings, and metaphors for powerful movements.
  • 将余之赠，以佐真主，施功立业，勉之，勉之！ - Encourages action by giving gifts to assist the true ruler and establish achievements.
  • 此后十余年，东南数千里外有异事，是吾得意之秋也 - Predicts unusual events in the southeast thousands of miles away, which will be a time of success.
  • 一妹与李郎可沥酒相贺 - Suggests a celebratory moment where the younger sister and Li Lang can toast each other.
  • 复回命家童列拜，曰：“李郎、一妹，是汝主也。可善事之！” - Gives instructions to the household servants to serve Li Lang and the younger sister well.
  • 言讫，与其妻戎服乘马，一奴从后，数步遂不复见 - Describes the departure of Qiu Rán and his wife, dressed in military attire, with a servant following.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虬髯 - Refers to a person with a dragon-like beard, often associated with a legendary figure.
  • 太原李氏 - Refers to the Li family of Taiyuan, a prominent family in Chinese history.
  • 龙战 - Refers to a metaphorical battle, often used to describe intense competition or struggle.
  • 虎啸风生，龙腾云合 - Metaphors for significant events or movements, often used to describe the rise of powerful figures or dynasties.
  • 真主 - Refers to a true ruler or leader, often used in a religious or political context.
  • 东南数千里外 - Refers to a distant region in the southeast, often used to describe a far-off place.

Proper Nouns

  • 虬髯 - A person with a dragon-like beard.
  • 靖 - The name of the person being addressed.
  • 太原李氏 - The Li family of Taiyuan.
  • 李郎 - The name of a person, likely a member of the Li family.
  • 一妹 - The younger sister, likely a member of the Li family.
  • 东南数千里外 - A distant region in the southeast.

[812]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂為豪家 - Transition from personal possession to social status; 'thus became a wealthy family.'
  • 得以助文皇缔构之资，遂匡大业 - Assistance provided to Emperor Wen; 'he was able to provide assistance with the resources to help Emperor Wen construct his cause, thereby supporting the grand endeavor.'
  • 人臣之谬思乱者，乃螳臂之拒走轮耳 - Metaphor for futile resistance; 'those ministers who foolishly think of rebellion are like the mantis blocking the wheel.'
  • 我皇家垂福万叶，岂虚然哉 - Confidence in imperial prosperity; 'our imperial family's blessings will last for generations, is this not true?'
  • 或曰：“卫国公之兵法，半是虬髯所传也。” - Historical connection; 'some say: 'The military strategies of Wei Guo Gong are half inherited from Qiu Ran.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 靖 - Refers to Li Jing, a famous general during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 文皇 - Refers to Emperor Wen of Tang (Li Shimin), the second emperor of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 贞观 - The era name of Emperor Taizong (Li Shimin) from 627 to 649.
  • 南蛮 - Refers to the southern barbarians, often used to describe tribes or states in the southern regions.
  • 扶苏国 - A fictional or historical state mentioned in the text, possibly a reference to a specific region or kingdom.
  • 虬髯 - Refers to Qiu Ran, a legendary figure known for his long beard and military prowess.
  • 卫国公 - A title given to Li Jing, indicating his high rank and contributions to the Tang Dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 靖 - Li Jing
  • 文皇 - Emperor Wen of Tang (Li Shimin)
  • 贞观 - The era name of Emperor Taizong (Li Shimin)
  • 南蛮 - Southern barbarians
  • 扶苏国 - A fictional or historical state
  • 虬髯 - Qiu Ran
  • 卫国公 - Li Jing

[814]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 明年 - Literally 'next year,' used to denote the following year.
  • 禮部侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Rites, one of the six ministries in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 耀上第 - To be awarded the top rank in an imperial examination; '耀' suggests being honored or distinguished.
  • 屏居問歲 - A phrase that is somewhat ambiguous. '屏居' may mean to live in seclusion or to be in a remote place. '問歲' could mean to inquire about the year or to wait for the year to pass.
  • 榮名及親 - 'Fame and honor reaching one’s parents,' referring to the traditional Confucian ideal of filial piety and honor through one’s achievements.
  • 昔人所尚 - 'What was esteemed by people of old,' indicating a traditional or classical value.
  • 濯泥之賤 - A metaphor for a lowly or dirty status, possibly referring to a person of low social rank or moral corruption.
  • 稽采蘭之美 - 'Delaying the beauty of picking orchids,' a metaphor for delaying or hindering something pure or noble.
  • 物器資用 - 'Supplies and necessities,' referring to material goods.
  • 足以待君之來也 - 'Sufficient to await your coming,' indicating that the resources are enough to wait for the person’s return.
  • 省家於清池 - 'To visit one’s family in Qingchi,' referring to returning home or visiting relatives.
  • 乏食 - 'Lack of food,' indicating poverty or scarcity.
  • 鬻妝具以自給 - 'Sold her ornaments to support herself,' indicating financial hardship.
  • 天寶未 - 'Before the Tianbao era,' referring to the reign of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang before the year Tianbao (742–756 CE).
  • 盜覆二京 - 'Bandits overthrew the two capitals,' referring to the An Lushan Rebellion, which led to the fall of Chang’an and Luoyang.
  • 士民奔駭 - 'Scholars and commoners fled in panic,' indicating the chaos and fear during the rebellion.
  • 剪發毀形 - 'Cut her hair and disfigured herself,' a method of hiding one’s identity or avoiding capture.
  • 寄跡法靈寺 - 'Hid her identity at Faling Temple,' indicating that she took refuge in a Buddhist temple.
  • 侯希逸 - A general and later warlord during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 自平盧節度淄青名 - 'From Pinglu, the military governor of Ziqing (Zi Qing),' indicating his military title and region.
  • 請為書記 - 'Requested to be his secretary,' a position in a military or administrative office.
  • 洎宣皇帝以神武反正 - 'When Emperor Xuanzong (the '宣皇帝') restored order through divine military prowess,' referring to the suppression of the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 遣使間行求柳氏 - 'Sent envoys secretly to seek out Liu Shi,' indicating a covert mission.
  • 練囊盛麸金 - 'A silk pouch containing flour-gold,' likely referring to a pouch of gold dust or fine gold, possibly a poetic or metaphorical expression.
  • 章台柳 - A well-known poetic reference to a woman, possibly a nickname or poetic allusion to Liu Shi, derived from the famous line '章台柳，章台柳，昔日青青今在否？' (The willow of Zhangtai, the willow of Zhangtai, do you still stand green as before?)
  • 縱使長條似舊垂 - 'Even if its long branches still hang as before,' a metaphor for the willow tree, symbolizing Liu Shi.
  • 亦應攀折他人手 - 'It should be plucked by another’s hand,' suggesting that she may have been taken by someone else.
  • 柳氏捧金嗚咽 - 'Liu Shi held the gold and wept,' indicating emotional distress.
  • 左右凄悯 - 'Those around her felt sorrowful,' indicating the emotional impact of the scene.
  • 答之曰 - 'She replied,' indicating a poetic or literary response.
  • 芳菲節 - 'The season of fragrance and bloom,' referring to spring or a time of beauty.
  • 所恨年年贈離別 - 'What I regret is that year after year it is given to partings,' a poetic expression of sorrow over repeated separations.
  • 一葉隨風忽報秋 - 'A single leaf, carried by the wind, suddenly announces autumn,' a metaphor for the passage of time or the coming of sorrow.
  • 縱使君來豈堪折 - 'Even if you return, how could I bear to be plucked?' indicating reluctance to be taken or reunited under painful circumstances.
  • 番將沙吒利 - 'The foreign general Sha Zali,' a name of a non-Han general during the Tang Dynasty.
  • 初立功 - 'Just beginning to establish his military reputation,' indicating a rising general.
  • 竊知柳氏之色 - 'Learned of Liu Shi’s beauty secretly,' indicating he discovered her beauty without her knowledge.
  • 劫以歸第 - 'Kidnapped her and brought her back to his residence,' indicating abduction.
  • 寵之專房 - 'Made her his sole concubine,' indicating he took her as his favorite mistress.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 禮部侍郎 - A high-ranking official in the Ministry of Rites, responsible for imperial examinations and rituals.
  • 天寶 - The era name of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang (r. 712–756), a period of prosperity before the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 二京 - Refers to the two capitals of the Tang Dynasty: Chang’an (modern Xi’an) and Luoyang.
  • 法靈寺 - A Buddhist temple in Luoyang, where Liu Shi sought refuge.
  • 侯希逸 - A military governor and general during the late Tang Dynasty, known for his role in the power struggles after the An Lushan Rebellion.
  • 宣皇帝 - A title for Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, who was known as '宣宗' in later dynasties, but here it likely refers to his reign name '宣' (Xuan).
  • 章台柳 - A famous poetic reference to a woman, likely a metaphor for Liu Shi. This phrase is from a well-known poem by the Tang poet Yin Zhen, which is a lament for a lost love.
  • 番將沙吒利 - 'The foreign general Sha Zali,' possibly a Turkic or Uyghur general who served under the Tang and later became a warlord.
  • 專房 - A term used to describe a concubine or favored mistress, often indicating exclusive favor.

[815]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 及希逸除左仆射 - When Xi Yi was appointed as Left Censor; '除' means 'to be appointed' or 'to assume office.'
  • 人觐 - People came to pay homage; '觐' means 'to pay homage' or 'to visit the emperor.'
  • 偶于龙首冈 - He happened to be at Longshou Hill; '偶' means 'to happen upon' or 'to chance upon.'
  • 以牛驾辎 - Driving a carriage with an ox; '辎' refers to a type of carriage.
  • 得非韩员外乎 - Is it not Mr. Han?; '得非' is a rhetorical question meaning 'is it not.'
  • 当遂永诀 - I shall part from you forever; '永诀' means 'parting forever' or 'farewell forever.'
  • 强应之 - He reluctantly agreed; '强' means 'reluctantly' or 'against his will.'
  • 抚剑言曰 - He drew his sword and said; '抚剑' means 'to draw one's sword' or 'to brandish a sword.'
  • 请足下数字 - Please write a few characters; '足下' is a respectful term for 'you.'
  • 径造沙吒利之第 - He went directly to the residence of Sha Zali; '径造' means 'to go directly to' or 'to proceed to.'
  • 犯关排闼 - He broke through the gates; '犯关' means 'to break through the gates' and '排闼' means 'to push open the door.'
  • 急趋而呼曰 - He rushed in and shouted; '急趋' means 'to rush' or 'to hurry' and '呼曰' means 'to shout and say.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 左仆射 - A high-ranking official in the imperial government, equivalent to a vice-premier.
  • 龙首冈 - A specific location, possibly a hill or a place of significance.
  • 沙吒利 - A person, likely a powerful figure or a military leader.
  • 虞侯 - A military officer or a guard, often responsible for protecting important figures.
  • 许俊 - A person, likely a brave and skilled individual.

Proper Nouns

  • 希逸 - Xi Yi, a historical figure.
  • 龙首冈 - Longshou Hill, a specific location.
  • 沙吒利 - Sha Zali, a person.
  • 许俊 - Xu Jun, a person.

[816]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂升堂 - Formal expression indicating entering a private or official space.
  • 出札示柳氏 - '札' can mean a summons, a document, or a letter.
  • 挟之跨鞍马 - Vivid and somewhat poetic expression indicating forceful or decisive action.
  • 逸尘断鞅 - Compound metaphor meaning 'flying dust and broken reins' – describing the rapid and powerful movement of the horse.
  • 倏忽乃至 - Expression of speed and suddenness.
  • 引裾而前曰 - Gesture of humility or respect before speaking.
  • 幸不辱命 - Formal expression of successful completion of a task.
  • 四座惊叹 - Standard expression used to describe the reaction of an audience.
  • 相与罢酒 - Indicating the end of a banquet or celebration.
  • 检校尚书金部员外郎兼御史 - Full official title: 'Inspector of the Ministry of Revenue, Assistant to the Ministry of Finance, and Inspector (御史)' – a complex bureaucratic title.
  • 累彰勋效 - Repeatedly demonstrated merit and effectiveness – a standard bureaucratic expression.
  • 阻绝凶寇 - '凶寇' refers to bandits or enemies.
  • 依止名尼 - '名尼' refers to a famous or respected nun.
  • 文明抚运 - In the era of civilizing rule and peaceful governance – a political phrase indicating a time of stability.
  • 遐迹率化 - Far-reaching traces have been civilized – a metaphor for widespread transformation or cultural progress.
  • 凶恣挠法 - Wickedly and recklessly defying the law – describing the behavior of the general.
  • 驱有志之妾，干无为之政 - Driving a virtuous concubine to engage in a useless political matter – a critique of the general's actions.
  • 义切中抱 - The sense of justice was deeply felt – indicating strong moral motivation.
  • 虽昭感激之诚 - Although the sincerity of gratitude was evident – showing acknowledgment of gratitude.
  • 事不先闻 - The matter was not previously known – indicating lack of prior communication.
  • 固乏训齐之令 - Thus lacking the command to train and discipline – a bureaucratic or military phrase.
  • 寻有诏 - Soon there was an imperial decree – a standard expression for the arrival of imperial orders.
  • 宜还韩 - Should be returned to Han – indicating the official decision.
  • 钦赐钱二百万 - Was granted two million coins as a reward – a formal expression of imperial reward.
  • 累迁至中书舍人 - Repeatedly promoted to the position of Secretary of the Secretariat – indicating a career advancement.
  • 志防闲而不克者也 - A person who aspired to restraint but failed – indicating a character flaw or failure in self-control.
  • 慕感激而不达者也 - A person who admired gratitude but failed to achieve anything – indicating unfulfilled potential.
  • 向使柳氏以色选 - If Liu Shi had been selected by virtue of her beauty – a conditional statement.
  • 辞熊辇之诚可继 - Could have continued the sincerity of the bear-carriage (a metaphor for loyalty or service) – a metaphorical expression.
  • 以才举 - If selected by talent – a standard bureaucratic expression.
  • 曹柯渑池之功可建 - Could have built the achievements of Cao Ke and the Battle of Mianchi – a reference to historical military achievements.
  • 事由迹彰 - Matters are made evident by their traces – a philosophical statement on cause and effect.
  • 功待事立 - Achievements await the establishment of events – indicating that accomplishments depend on action.
  • 惜郁堙不偶 - Alas, their talents were stifled and not matched – a lament over unfulfilled potential.
  • 义勇徒激 - Their sense of justice and courage were only stirred – indicating unfulfilled action.
  • 皆不入于正 - None of them entered the path of righteousness – a moral judgment.
  • 斯岂变之正乎 - Is this the correct way of change? – a rhetorical question.
  • 盖所遇然也 - It was due to the circumstances they encountered – a conclusion on the role of fate or opportunity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 柳氏 - Liu Shi – a woman, likely a concubine or wife, central to the narrative.
  • 许俊 - Xu Jun – a military official or general, key to the plot.
  • 韩 - Han – likely a high-ranking official or noble, the husband of Liu Shi.
  • 沙吁利 - Sha Xuli – a military general or officer, the antagonist in the narrative.
  • 检校尚书金部员外郎兼御史 - Full title of a high-ranking official: 'Inspector of the Ministry of Revenue, Assistant to the Ministry of Finance, and Inspector (御史)' – a complex bureaucratic title from the Tang or later dynasties.
  • 中书舍人 - Secretary of the Secretariat – a high-ranking official in the central government, responsible for drafting imperial edicts.
  • 熊辇 - Bear-carriage – a metaphor for loyalty or service, possibly referencing a historical or literary allusion.
  • 曹柯渑池 - Cao Ke and Mianchi – historical references to battles or military figures. '渑池' (Mianchi) refers to the Battle of Mianchi, a famous historical event involving the states of Qin and Zhao. '曹柯' (Cao Ke) is likely a reference to a historical figure or a place.
  • 文明抚运 - In the era of civilizing rule and peaceful governance – a term used to describe a period of stability or enlightened rule, possibly from the Tang or Song dynasties.
  • 训齐之令 - Command to train and discipline – a military or bureaucratic term indicating orders for training or discipline.

Proper Nouns

  • 柳氏 - Liu Shi – a woman, likely a concubine or wife, central to the narrative.
  • 许俊 - Xu Jun – a military official or general, key to the plot.
  • 韩 - Han – likely a high-ranking official or noble, the husband of Liu Shi.
  • 沙吁利 - Sha Xuli – a military general or officer, the antagonist in the narrative.
  • 检校尚书金部员外郎兼御史 - Full title of a high-ranking official: 'Inspector of the Ministry of Revenue, Assistant to the Ministry of Finance, and Inspector (御史)' – a complex bureaucratic title.

[817]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 戲弄相狎 - Describes the playful and close relationship between children, reflecting traditional family dynamics.
  • 奉孀姊及抚仙客尤至 - Emphasizes the filial piety and care shown by Liu Zhen towards his widowed sister and nephew, reflecting Confucian values.
  • 恨不见其婚宦 - Expresses a common concern in traditional Chinese families about the marriage prospects of their children.
  • 端丽聪慧 - Describes the ideal qualities of a woman in traditional Chinese culture, emphasizing beauty and intelligence.
  • 异日无令归他族 - Reflects the desire to keep family ties strong and the importance of marriage within the family, a common theme in traditional Chinese society.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 唐玉仙客 - The name of the protagonist, Xianke, who is a nephew of the official Liu Zhen.
  • 建中 - The era name of Emperor Dezong of Tang, indicating the historical period.
  • 外氏 - The paternal family of the mother's side, a term used in traditional Chinese family structures.
  • 襄郡 - A place name, likely referring to a region in the Tang dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 劉震 - The official who is the protagonist's uncle.
  • 無雙 - The daughter of Liu Zhen, the main female character.

[818]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 时震为尚书租庸使 - At that time, Shen was serving as the Supervisor of Rent and Labor.
  • 门馆赫奕 - His household was grand and impressive.
  • 冠盖填塞 - The roads were crowded with officials and dignitaries.
  • 置于学舍，弟子为伍 - He was placed in the school building, among the students.
  • 舅甥之分，依然如故 - The relationship between uncle and nephew remained as it was.
  • 寂然不闻选取之议 - There was no mention of any selection or choice.
  • 于窗隙间窥见无双 - He saw her through a crack in the window, and she was without equal.
  • 姿质明艳，若神仙中人 - Her beauty was radiant and she seemed like a celestial being.
  • 仙客发狂，惟恐姻亲之事不谐矣 - The guest from the fairy realm went mad, fearing that the marriage alliance would not be successful.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 士人 - A 'scholar-official,' a member of the scholar-gentry class in classical China, often aspiring to government service.
  • 骏骑 - A 'fine horse,' used as a symbol of power and status in classical Chinese literature.
  • 宫苑 - 'Palace gardens' or 'imperial gardens,' indicating a royal or imperial setting.
  • 深坑 - 'Deep pit,' a common motif in classical Chinese literature for imprisonment, punishment, or magical confinement.
  • 绢 - 'Silk,' a valuable and refined material, often used in classical Chinese for clothing, binding, or magical purposes.
  • 官城 - 'Imperial city' or 'palace city,' indicating the location of the palace or government.

  • 愈益恶之，夫不胜其忧 - She grew increasingly hateful, and her husband could not bear his worries. Conveys deepening resentment and distress.

  • 吾病且死，非药饵能已 - I am dying of my illness, and no medicine can cure it. Direct statement of despair.

  • 其夫自河间病，言如狂人 - Her husband, who had been ill in Hejian, spoke like a madman. Suggests a mental or emotional breakdown.

  • 既张具，河间命邑臣，告其夫召鬼祝诅上 - After setting up the ritual, the officials from Hejian reported that she had summoned a ghost to curse the emperor. Specific accusation with serious consequences.
  • 下吏讯验，笞杀之 - The officials interrogated and verified, then beat her to death. Describes a brutal and swift punishment.
  • 将死犹曰：“吾负夫人，吾负夫人。” - As she was dying, she said, 'I have wronged my wife, I have wronged my wife.' Final statement adds a layer of moral complexity.
  • 河间大喜，不为服，开门召所与淫者 - The officials from Hejian were very pleased, did not mourn for her, and opened the door to call in those she had been intimate with. Shows a lack of respect and a focus on personal gratification.
  • 倮逐为荒淫 - They chased them naked and engaged in debauchery. Describes a scene of moral degradation.
  • 居一岁，所淫者衰，益厌，乃出之 - After a year, those she had been intimate with declined, and she grew even more disgusted, so she sent them away. Shows a shift in her behavior.
  • 召长安无赖男子，晨夜交于门 - She called in worthless men from Chang'an, engaging in intercourse day and night at the door. Continues the theme of moral decay.
  • 犹不慊 - Still, she was not satisfied. Indicates ongoing dissatisfaction.
  • 又为酒垆西南隅，己居楼上微观之 - She also set up a wine shop in the southwest corner, living upstairs and watching them. Shows her continued involvement in this lifestyle.
  • 凿小门，以女侍饵焉 - She dug a small door, and had female attendants lure them in. Describes a calculated and manipulative approach.
  • 凡来饮酒大鼻者，少且壮者，美颜色者，善为戏酒者，皆上与合，且合且窥 - Anyone who came to drink, with a big nose, young and strong, beautiful appearance, or skilled at playing with wine, would all be with her, and she would watch them. Lists the types of men she found attractive and her method of attraction.
  • 恐失一男子也，犹日呻呼懵懵，以为不足 - She feared losing even one man, and still moaned and groaned every day, thinking it was not enough. Conveys her obsessive and insatiable nature.
  • 积十余年，病髓竭而死 - After more than ten years, she died from exhaustion of her marrow. Describes a prolonged and ultimately fatal condition.
  • 自是虽戚里为邪行者，闻河间之名，则掩鼻蹙额，皆不欲道也 - From then on, even those in the nobility who engaged in immoral behavior, when they heard of the name of Hejian, would cover their noses and wrinkle their brows, and did not want to mention it. Shows the lasting negative impact of her actions.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 女泣而从之 - Can be read as 'the girl wept and followed him' or 'the girl wept while following him.'
  • 生携归逆旅，匿小楼中 - Can be read as 'the man brought her back to an inn and hid her in a small tower' or 'the man brought her back to an inn and hid her in a small building.'
  • 女自言曹氏 - Can be read as 'the girl introduced herself as a member of the Cao family' or 'the girl said she was from the Cao family.'
  • 父早丧，独有己一女，母钟爱之 - Can be read as 'her father died early, leaving only her one daughter, whom her mother doted on' or 'her father died early, and she had only one daughter, whom her mother doted on.'
  • 为择所归 - Can be read as 'her mother chose a suitable match for her' or 'her mother chose a suitable place for her to go.'
  • 素悦姑之子某 - Can be read as 'she had always liked her uncle's son, someone' or 'she had always liked her uncle's son, whose name is unknown.'
  • 私约相奔 - Can be read as 'they made a private agreement to elope' or 'they made a private agreement to run away together.'
  • 墙下微叹而去者 - Can be read as 'those who sighed softly and left by the wall' or 'those who sighed softly and left through the wall.'
  • 生既南宫不利 - Can be read as 'the man did not do well in the southern palace' or 'the man did not succeed in the southern palace.'
  • 迁延数月，无归意 - Can be read as 'he lingered for several months, showing no intention to return' or 'he stayed for several months, showing no intention to go back.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 友款于门 - Classical expression meaning 'a friend arrived at the gate' or 'a guest arrived at the door'; '款' means 'to visit' or 'to arrive.'
  • 即有小童应客 - 'Immediately a small child responded to the guest'; '应客' is a classical way of saying 'to greet a guest' or 'to attend to a visitor.'
  • 姿状秀美 - 'Appearance and bearing were beautiful and refined'; '姿状' refers to one's appearance and demeanor.
  • 进趋安详 - 'Moved with calm and dignity'; '进趋' refers to the manner of approaching or moving, and '安详' means calm or composed.
  • 殊有富贵家气 - 'Clearly had the air of a wealthy family'; '殊' means 'especially' or 'clearly,' and '富贵家气' describes someone with the bearing of nobility or wealth.
  • 揖客曰 - 'He bowed to the guest and said'; '揖' is a formal bow used in classical Chinese to greet or show respect.
  • 久别甚思款接，都不见过，何也？ - 'I have long been away and greatly desired to entertain you, yet you did not come, why is that?' A classical way of expressing longing for a meeting.
  • 揖楼 - 'He bowed to the tower' or 'he bowed to the person on the tower'; the phrase is somewhat ambiguous, possibly referring to a person on a tower or a place.
  • 吾亲也 - 'He is my relative'; a straightforward statement, though the relationship is not specified.
  • 款语十刻许 - 'They conversed for about ten moments'; '款语' is a classical term for 'friendly conversation,' and '十刻' refers to a unit of time, though its exact duration is uncertain.
  • 日旰 - 'The sun has declined' or 'the day is late'; a classical way of saying it is late in the day.
  • 二君馁乎 - 'Are the two of you hungry?'; '馁' is a classical term for hunger or being famished.
  • 推西边小户 - 'Pushed open the small door on the west side'; '户' is a door, and '小户' refers to a small or side door.
  • 华屋三间 - 'Three elegant rooms' or 'three fine houses'; '华屋' is a classical term for a beautiful or luxurious house.
  • 窗几如拭 - 'The windows and tables were as clean as if just wiped'; a classical way of saying the room was spotless.
  • 玩具皆珍奇 - 'The toys were all rare and precious'; '玩具' is a classical term for 'toys' or 'decorative objects.'

  • 楼观森列 - 'Towers and pavilions stood in dense rows'; '楼观' refers to buildings, and '森列' is a classical term for 'standing in dense rows.'
  • 夕阳映照 - 'The setting sun illuminated'; a classical way of describing the sun setting.
  • 丹碧紫翠 - 'Red, blue, purple, and green'; these are classical color terms.
  • 互相发明 - 'Interacted and illuminated each other'; a classical way of saying the colors enhanced each other.
  • 渔歌菱唱 - 'The songs of fishermen and lotus gatherers'; '菱唱' refers to the songs of people harvesting lotus, a classical image.
  • 隐隐在耳 - 'Faintly in the ears'; a classical way of describing distant sounds.

  • 二美人径出 - 'Two beautiful women came straight out'; '径出' is a classical way of saying 'came directly out' or 'emerged suddenly.'
  • 靓妆丽色 - 'Elaborate makeup and beautiful colors'; a classical way of describing a woman's appearance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 友款于门 - A classical way of describing a guest arriving at someone’s home. In Chinese literature, this often sets the stage for supernatural or fantastical encounters.
  • 少年僧 - A young monk. This is a common character in classical Chinese fiction, often representing a mysterious or supernatural figure.
  • 姿状秀美，进趋安详，殊有富贵家气 - This description of the monk suggests a contrast between his monastic appearance and noble bearing. Such contrasts are common in classical Chinese fiction, especially in stories involving supernatural beings or immortal figures.
  • 日旰 - A classical expression used to denote the time of day, often used in stories to indicate the passage of time or to set a scene.
  • 素膳三品 - The term '素膳' refers to vegetarian or plain food, which is often used in classical Chinese literature to describe the food of monks or ascetics. The fact that it is described as '甘好精美' suggests that it is not just plain but also exquisite, which may be a metaphor or a supernatural element.
  • 平湖当前，数十百顷 - This is a classical image of a vast lake, often used in Chinese literature to evoke serenity, beauty, or a dreamlike setting.
  • 楼观森列 - A common description of a grand or supernatural landscape, often found in Daoist or Buddhist-inspired stories.
  • 夕阳映照，丹碧紫翠，互相发明 - This is a classical way of describing a colorful, natural scene, often used in poetic or descriptive passages to create a vivid and harmonious image.
  • 渔歌菱唱 - These are traditional sounds of rural life, often used to evoke a peaceful or idyllic setting. The inclusion of such sounds in a supernatural or dreamlike scene may indicate a transition between the real and the otherworldly.
  • 小画肪 - A small, ornate boat. This is a common image in classical Chinese literature, often used to depict a journey or a romantic or supernatural encounter.
  • 二美人径出 - The appearance of two beautiful women is a common trope in classical Chinese fiction, especially in stories involving immortals, spirits, or otherworldly beings.
  • 靓妆丽色，王公家不过也 - This is a classical way of describing beauty that surpasses even the nobility. Such descriptions often indicate that the women are not ordinary humans, but perhaps spirits or immortals.
  • 觞豆罗陈，穷极水陆 - This is a classical way of describing a lavish feast, often found in stories involving supernatural beings or otherworldly settings.

  • 一彈既罷還一彈，願似流泉鎮相續 - Describes the continuous flow of the music, wishing it to continue like a never-ending stream of water.

  • 李丞相逢吉 - Refers to Li Fengji, a chancellor during the Tang Dynasty known for his cunning and jealousy.
  • 劉禹錫 - Refers to Liu Yuxi, a prominent poet and statesman of the Tang Dynasty.

[907]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 元稹微之 - Reference to Yuan Zhen, a prominent Tang Dynasty poet, often referred to by his courtesy name 'Wei Zhi'.
  • 知有薛涛，未尝识面 - Knows of Xue Tao but has never met her face to face, indicating prior knowledge without personal acquaintance.
  • 初授监察御史，出使西蜀 - Initially appointed as a censor, sent on a mission to Shu (Sichuan), describing Yuan Zhen's official duties.
  • 得与薛涛相见 - Got to meet Xue Tao, marking the first personal encounter.
  • 自后元公赴京，薛涛归浣花所 - Afterwards, Yuan Zhen went to the capital, and Xue Tao returned to Huahua (her residence), indicating their separation.
  • 多造十色彩笺 - Often produced ten-colored decorative papers, referring to the production of colorful paper.
  • 涛别模新样小幅松花纸 - Xue Tao created a new style of small-sized pine flower paper, describing a specific type of paper she made.
  • 多用题诗，因寄献元公百余幅 - Used it frequently for writing poetry and sent over a hundred pieces to Yuan Zhen, indicating the purpose and quantity of the paper sent.
  • 元于松花纸上，寄赠一篇曰 - Yuan Zhen wrote a poem on the pine flower paper and sent it, indicating the poetic response.
  • 锦江滑腻岷峨秀 - The brocade river is smooth, and Mount Min and Mount E are beautiful, a poetic description of the natural scenery.
  • 幻作文君及薛涛 - Imagining Wen Jun and Xue Tao, a reference to the beauty of the women.
  • 言语巧偷鹦鹉舌 - Words are skillfully stolen from the parrot's tongue, a metaphor for eloquent speech.
  • 文章分得凤凰毛 - Writing shares the phoenix's feathers, a metaphor for beautiful writing.
  • 纷纷词客皆停笔 - Many poets stop writing, indicating the high regard for Xue Tao's work.
  • 个个公侯欲梦刀 - Every nobleman desires to dream of a sword, a metaphor for aspiring to high office.
  • 别后相思隔烟水 - After separation, longing is separated by mist and water, a poetic expression of longing.
  • 富蒲花发五云高 - The rich lotus flowers bloom, reaching the height of five clouds, a poetic description of the lotus flowers.
  • 薛尝好种菖蒲 - Xue Tao used to love planting calamus, indicating her interest in gardening.
  • 罚赴边，有怀上韦相公云 - Sent to the border as punishment, he wrote a poem to Wei Xiang Gong (a high-ranking official), indicating the context of the poem.
  • 闻道边城苦，而今到始知 - Hearing that the border is harsh, only now do I know, a reflection on the hardships of the border.
  • 却将门下曲，唱与陇头儿 - But I will sing the songs of the court to the children of the border, a metaphor for sharing culture.
  • 元微之赠涛诗，因寄旧诗与之云 - Yuan Wei Zhi wrote a poem to Xue Tao and sent it along with an old poem, indicating the exchange of poetry.
  • 诗篇调态人皆有，细腻风光我独知 - Everyone has poems with different styles, but I alone know the delicate beauty, a reflection on personal taste.
  • 月夜咏花怜暗淡，雨朝题柳为欹垂 - On moonlit nights, I praise flowers for their dimness; on rainy mornings, I write about willows leaning, a poetic description of nature.
  • 老大不能收拾得，与君开似教男儿 - As I grow old, I can't collect everything, but I will show it to you as if teaching a boy, a reflection on aging and sharing knowledge.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 薛涛好制小诗 - Literal meaning: 'Xue Tao liked to compose short poems.' Sets the context for Xue Tao's literary preferences.
  • 惜其幅大，狭小之 - Literal meaning: 'she was dissatisfied with the size of the paper and made it smaller.' Indicates her innovation in creating smaller paper for poetry.
  • 蜀中号薛涛笺 - Literal meaning: 'In Shu (Sichuan), it was called Xue Tao's paper.' Refers to the nickname given to the smaller paper she created.
  • 或以营妓无校书之号，韦南康欲奏之而罢，后遂呼之 - Ambiguous phrase likely meaning: 'Some people thought that as a courtesan, Xue Tao did not have the title of 'compiler' (校书), but Wei Nankang wanted to recommend her for the title, though it was not granted, and later people still called her by that name.' Highlights a historical misunderstanding or controversy.
  • 胡曾诗曰 - Literal meaning: 'Hu Zeng's poem says.' Indicates that the following is a poem by Hu Zeng.
  • 万里楼台女校书 - Poetic reference to Xue Tao as a 'female compiler' (校书) associated with the 'Ten-Thousand-Mile Tower' (万里楼台), symbolizing her literary status.
  • 扫眉才子知多少，领取春风总不如 - Poetic line meaning: 'How many talented women have swept their brows (a metaphor for writing), but none can match the grace of Xue Tao in the spring breeze.' Emphasizes Xue Tao's exceptional talent.
  • 进士杨蕴中，下成都狱 - Literal meaning: 'Jinshi Yang Yunzhong was imprisoned in Chengdu.' Introduces a historical event.
  • 赠诗云 - Literal meaning: 'He gave her a poem.' Indicates that the following is a poem given by the woman in the dream.
  • 玉漏声长灯耿耿，东墙西墙时见影 - Poetic line describing the dream, possibly symbolizing the passage of time or the woman's presence.
  • 月明窗外子规啼，忍使孤魂愁夜永 - Another poetic line, likely expressing sorrow or longing in the dream.
  • 张建封妓 - Literal meaning: 'Zhang Jianfeng's courtesans.' Sets the context for the following story.
  • 其序云 - Literal meaning: 'The preface says.' Indicates that the following is the preface or introduction to Bai Juyi's poem.
  • 徐州张尚书，有爱妓盼盼，善歌舞，雅多风态 - Describes Zhang Shangfu's beloved courtesan Pan Pan, who was skilled in singing, dancing, and had a graceful demeanor.
  • 予为校书郎时，游淮泗间，张尚书宴予，酒酣，出盼盼佐欢 - Literal meaning: 'When I was a compiler (校书郎), I traveled in the Huai and Si regions, and Zhang Shangfu entertained me with a banquet. When we were drunk, he brought out Pan Pan to entertain us.' Sets the scene of Bai Juyi's encounter with Pan Pan.
  • 昨日，司勋员外郎张仲素绘之访予，因吟新诗，有《燕子楼》诗三首，辞甚婉丽 - Literal meaning: 'Yesterday, the Deputy Minister of Rites Zhang Zhongsu visited me and recited a new poem, 'Yanzi Pavilion,' which consists of three verses and is very graceful and beautiful.' Introduces Zhang Zhongsu's visit and the poem.
  • 诘其由，乃盼盼所作也 - Literal meaning: 'When I asked him about the origin, he said it was written by Pan Pan.' Reveals the authorship of the poem.
  • 绘之从事武宁累年，颇知盼盼始未 - Literal meaning: 'Zhang Zhongsu has served in Wuning for many years and knows quite a bit about Pan Pan's past.' Provides context for Zhang Zhongsu's knowledge of Pan Pan.
  • 盼有诗云 - Literal meaning: 'Pan Pan wrote a poem.' Indicates that the following is a poem by Pan Pan.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 殘燈伴曉霜 - A poetic expression meaning 'a dim lamp accompanied by the morning frost,' evoking a scene of solitude and melancholy.
  • 獨眠人起合歡床 - 'A lonely sleeper rises from the bed of joy,' where '合歡床' (bed of joy) is a euphemism for a marital bed, suggesting a contrast between the bed's name and the current solitude.
  • 相思一夜知多少 - A rhetorical question meaning 'how much longing does one know in a night of thought,' expressing the depth of emotional distress.
  • 地角天涯不是長 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'the ends of the earth and the far corners of the sky are not long,' implying that even the vastest distances cannot match the intensity of longing.
  • 燕子樓中思悄然 - 'In the Swallow Tower, thoughts are silent and profound,' where '燕子樓' (Swallow Tower) is a specific location associated with a story of longing and separation.
  • 自埋劍履歌塵散 - 'Since burying the sword and shoes, the songs and dust have dispersed,' a reference to the death of a lover and the fading of their memory.
  • 紅袖香銷一十年 - 'Red sleeves have faded after ten years,' a metaphor for the passage of time and the fading of beauty or memory.
  • 滿窗明月滿帘霜 - 'The full window is bathed in moonlight, and the curtain is covered in frost,' evoking a scene of solitude and coldness.
  • 被冷灯残拂卧床 - 'The quilt is cold, the lamp is dim, and the bed is brushed,' describing the physical discomfort of solitude.
  • 所以偷生尔 - 'That is why I have survived,' indicating the narrator's decision to live for the sake of her lord's reputation.
  • 黄金不惜买蛾眉，拣得如花四五枝 - 'Not sparing gold to buy beautiful women, selecting four or five like flowers,' describing the purchase of concubines.
  • 歌舞教成心力尽，一朝身去不相随 - 'Taught in dance and song until heart and strength are exhausted, yet one day the body departs without following,' expressing the fleeting nature of beauty and the inevitability of death.

  • 从死之妾 - Refers to a wife or concubine who commits suicide after the death of her husband or lord, a practice associated with the Tang Dynasty and the story of Bai Juyi and his concubine.
  • 蛾眉 - A metaphor for beautiful women, often used in classical Chinese poetry to describe concubines or mistresses.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自守空楼敛恨眉 - Literally means 'self-guarding an empty tower, frowning in hatred,' which describes a state of solitude and sorrow.
  • 形同春后牡丹枝 - A metaphor comparing the person's appearance to a withered branch of a peony after spring, symbolizing decline and loss of vitality.
  • 舍人不会人深意 - '舍人' (a term for a retainer or attendant) does not understand the deep meaning of the situation.
  • 讶道泉台不去随 - Surprised that she would not follow to the underworld (泉台), implying a misunderstanding of her intentions.
  • 怏怏旬日，不食而卒 - '怏怏' means feeling dejected or discontented; '旬日' is about ten days; '不食而卒' means she died without eating.
  • 儿童不识冲天物 - Children do not recognize something that reaches the heavens, suggesting something extraordinary or beyond their comprehension.
  • 谩把青泥污雪毫 - '谩' means carelessly or recklessly; '青泥' refers to greenish mud or ink; '雪毫' refers to white hair or a fine brush. The phrase suggests carelessly defiling something pure or noble.
  • 驱马渐觉远 - Riding a horse gradually feels distant, indicating a sense of growing separation.
  • 回头长路尘 - Looking back at the long road covered in dust, symbolizing the passage of time and distance.
  • 高城已不见，况复城中人 - The high city is no longer visible, let alone the people within it, emphasizing the vastness of separation.
  • 去意既未甘，居情谅多辛 - The intention to leave was not willingly accepted, and living in the current place must have been filled with much hardship.
  • 五原东北晋，千里西南秦 - Refers to the geographical locations of five original states in the northeast (Jin) and thousands of miles in the southwest (Qin), indicating a vast distance.
  • 一屡不出门，一车无停轮 - '一屡' means once or a single instance; '不出门' means not leaving the house; '一车无停轮' means a cart never stops moving, symbolizing constant motion.
  • 流萍与系匏 - '流萍' refers to floating duckweed, symbolizing aimlessness; '系匏' refers to tied gourds, symbolizing stability.
  • 早晚期相亲 - '早晚' means early or late; '相亲' means to be close or intimate. The phrase suggests that intimacy will come either early or late.
  • 自从别后减容光 - Since the separation, her appearance has diminished, indicating a loss of beauty or vitality.
  • 半是思郎半恨郎 - Half of her thoughts are of him, and half are filled with resentment, expressing complex emotions.
  • 欲识旧来云髻样 - To know the shape of her old cloud-like hairdo, referring to her previous elegant hairstyle.
  • 为奴开取镂金箱 - Ask the slave to open the intricately carved gold box, symbolizing a request for something precious or significant.

[911]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 绝笔而逝 - Literally 'to finish writing and then pass away,' this phrase suggests the author's death after completing a work.
  • 径持归京 - '径' means 'directly' or 'straightforwardly,' and '持归京' means 'to carry back to the capital.' Together, it conveys the action of directly bringing something back to the capital.
  • 具白其事 - '具' means 'completely' or 'in detail,' and '白' means 'to report' or 'to inform.' This phrase means 'to fully report the matter.'
  • 一恸而卒 - '一恸' means 'a single great cry of sorrow,' and '卒' means 'to die.' This phrase describes dying after a single intense expression of grief.
  • 以能文擢第 - '以' means 'because,' '能文' means 'capable of writing,' and '擢第' means 'to be promoted to a higher rank.' This phrase means 'to be promoted due to literary talent.'
  • 爰始一命 - '爰' means 'then' or 'thereupon,' '始' means 'beginning,' and '一命' refers to the first official appointment. This phrase means 'then received his first official appointment.'
  • 食大学之禄 - '食' means 'to receive,' '大学' refers to the Imperial Academy, and '禄' means 'salary' or 'stipend.' This phrase means 'to receive a salary from the Imperial Academy.'
  • 助成均之教 - '助' means 'to assist,' '成均' is an ancient name for the Imperial Academy, and '教' means 'education.' This phrase means 'to assist in the education at the Imperial Academy.'
  • 有庸绩矣 - '有' means 'to have,' '庸' means 'merit,' and '绩' means 'achievement.' This phrase means 'has achieved merit.'
  • 会东方军兴 - '会' means 'coincided with' or 'happened to be during,' '东方' refers to the eastern regions, and '军兴' means 'military campaign.' This phrase means 'coincided with a military campaign in the east.'
  • 府县未暇慰荐 - '府县' refers to the local government, '未暇' means 'had no time,' and '慰荐' means 'to console and recommend.' This phrase means 'the local government had no time to console and recommend him.'
  • 倦游太原 - '倦' means 'tired,' '游' means 'to travel,' and '太原' is a city in Shanxi Province. This phrase means 'tired of traveling to Taiyuan.'
  • 还来帝京 - '还' means 'return,' '来' means 'to come,' and '帝京' refers to the capital. This phrase means 'returned to the capital.'
  • 卒官灵台 - '卒' means 'to die,' '官' means 'official,' and '灵台' is an ancient observatory. This phrase means 'died while serving as an official at the observatory.'
  • 悲夫 - An expression of sorrow or regret, often used to introduce a lament.
  • 生于单贫 - '生于' means 'born into,' '单贫' means 'poverty.' This phrase means 'born into poverty.'
  • 以询名故 - '以' means 'because,' '询名' means 'fame,' and '故' means 'therefore.' This phrase means 'because of his fame.'
  • 心专勤俭 - '心专' means 'dedicated,' '勤俭' means 'diligent and frugal.' This phrase means 'dedicated to diligence and frugality.'
  • 不识声色 - '不识' means 'did not know,' '声色' refers to music and women. This phrase means 'did not indulge in music and women.'
  • 兹篮仕 - '兹' means 'this,' '篮' is likely a typo or misreading, possibly meant to be '初' (beginning), and '仕' means 'official career.' This phrase means 'at the beginning of his official career.'
  • 未知洞房纤腰之为蛊惑 - '未知' means 'did not know,' '洞房' refers to a bridal chamber, '纤腰' means 'slender waist,' and '蛊惑' means 'seductive.' This phrase means 'did not know the seductive allure of a bridal chamber and slender waist.'
  • 初抵太原 - '初' means 'initially,' '抵' means 'arrive,' and '太原' is a city in Shanxi Province. This phrase means 'initially arrived in Taiyuan.'
  • 居大将军宴席上 - '居' means 'to be present at,' '大将军' refers to a high-ranking military officer, and '宴席' means 'banquet.' This phrase means 'was present at a banquet hosted by a high-ranking military officer.'
  • 妓有此方之尤者 - '妓' means 'courtesan,' '此方' refers to the local area, and '尤者' means 'the best.' This phrase means 'a courtesan who was the best in the local area.'
  • 屡目于生 - '屡' means 'often,' '目' means 'to look at,' and '生' means 'the man.' This phrase means 'often looked at the man.'
  • 感悦之 - '感' means 'to be moved by,' '悦' means 'pleased,' and '之' is a particle. This phrase means 'was moved and pleased by her.'
  • 留赏累月 - '留' means 'to stay,' '赏' means 'to enjoy,' and '累月' means 'for several months.' This phrase means 'stayed and enjoyed for several months.'
  • 以为婉妾之乐 - '以为' means 'thought that,' '婉妾' means 'a gentle concubine,' and '乐' means 'joy.' This phrase means 'thought that the joy of a gentle concubine.'
  • 尽在是矣 - '尽' means 'all,' '在' means 'in,' and '是' means 'this.' This phrase means 'was all in this.'
  • 南辕 - '南' means 'south,' and '辕' means 'chariot.' This phrase means 'headed south.'
  • 请同行 - '请' means 'to ask,' '同' means 'together,' and '行' means 'to travel.' This phrase means 'asked to travel together.'
  • 十目所视 - '十目' means 'ten eyes,' and '所视' means 'what is seen.' This phrase means 'what is seen by many eyes.'
  • 辞焉 - '辞' means 'to refuse,' and '焉' is a particle. This phrase means 'refused.'
  • 请待至都而来迎 - '请' means 'to ask,' '待' means 'to wait,' '至都' means 'to the capital,' and '来迎' means 'to come and welcome.' This phrase means 'asked to wait until he reached the capital and then come and welcome him.'
  • 许之 - '许' means 'to agree,' and '之' is a particle. This phrase means 'agreed to it.'
  • 乃诀去 - '乃' means 'then,' '诀' means 'to part ways,' and '去' means 'to leave.' This phrase means 'then parted ways and left.'
  • 连蹇 - '连' means 'continuous,' and '蹇' means 'difficulties.' This phrase means 'continuous difficulties.'
  • 不克如约 - '克' means 'to fulfill,' '如' means 'as,' and '约' means 'agreement.' This phrase means 'could not fulfill the agreement.'
  • 过期 - '过' means 'past,' and '期' means 'deadline.' This phrase means 'past the deadline.'
  • 命甲遣乘密往迎妓 - '命' means 'to order,' '甲' refers to a person, '遣' means 'to send,' '乘' means 'vehicle,' '密' means 'secretly,' and '往迎妓' means 'to go and welcome the courtesan.' This phrase means 'ordered someone to secretly send a vehicle to go and welcome the courtesan.'
  • 积望成疾 - '积望' means 'accumulated longing,' and '成疾' means 'became ill.' This phrase means 'became ill due to accumulated longing.'
  • 不可为也 - '不可' means 'cannot,' '为' means 'do,' and '也' is a particle. This phrase means 'cannot be done.'
  • 先大故之夕 - '先' means 'before,' '大故' means 'great misfortune,' and '之夕' means 'that night.' This phrase means 'the night before the great misfortune.'
  • 剪其云髻 - '剪' means 'to cut,' '其' means 'her,' '云髻' means 'hair styled like clouds.' This phrase means 'cut her hair styled like clouds.'
  • 谓侍儿曰 - '谓' means 'to say to,' '侍儿' means 'maid,' and '曰' means 'said.' This phrase means 'said to the maid.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 韓退之 - Han Yu (768–824), a key figure in the Classical Prose Movement and a Tang dynasty scholar-official.
  • 何蕃書 - Letter to He Fan, a famous essay by Han Yu. It is a philosophical and moral reflection on the nature of virtue and the role of the scholar-official.
  • 歐陽詹 - A scholar of the Tang dynasty, known for his literary and philosophical works. He is mentioned in Han Yu’s Letter to He Fan as a person who lived a virtuous life and was deeply admired.
  • 河南穆玄道 - A person named Mu Xuandao from Henan. The name is likely a historical figure, though not widely known in classical literature.
  • 《華山畿》 - A yuefu poem about a tragic love story between a man and a woman. The name Huashanjī (華山畿) may refer to a location or a symbolic name.
  • 《玉台新詠》 - A poetic anthology compiled by Xu Ling in the Liang dynasty. It includes many poems about love and beauty.
  • 廬江小吏更相死 - Refers to the tragic story of two lovers, one a minor official from Lujiang and the other a woman, who died for each other. This is a well-known story in Chinese literature and is often compared to the Romeo and Juliet of Chinese tradition.
  • 古樂府詩 - The yuefu (樂府) was an official organization in the Han dynasty responsible for collecting folk songs and poems. These poems often reflected the lives and emotions of common people.

  • 中夜欲相從，嚴城限軍門 - They want to meet at night, but the strict city walls and military gates prevent it; '嚴城限軍門' (strict city walls and military gates) describes the obstacles they face.

  • 驚離腸千結，滴淚眼雙昏 - They are shocked and their hearts are in knots, and they cry; '驚離腸千結' (shocked and heart in knots) and '滴淚眼雙昏' (tears streaming down eyes) describe their emotional state.
  • 本朝達京師，回駕相追攀 - They both return to the capital, and he tries to catch up with her; '追攀' (catch up with) is an idiomatic expression describing his attempt to reconnect.
  • 宿約始乖阻，巧笑安能幹 - Their previous agreement is broken, and her clever smiles can no longer make things right; '宿約始乖阻' (previous agreement is broken) and '巧笑安能幹' (clever smiles can no longer make things right) describe the breakdown of their relationship.
  • 防身本苦節，一去何由還 - She has always been cautious and once she leaves, she will not return; '防身本苦節' (always cautious) and '一去何由還' (once she leaves, she will not return) are idiomatic expressions describing her character.

  • 折券从良 - 'Released from servitude and allowed to become a free person.' '折券' is a metaphor for freeing someone from a bond or debt, often used in the context of emancipating servants.'
  • 性便辟，巧言语 - 'She was smooth in manner and skilled in speech.' '便辟' means 'smooth and polite in manner,' often used to describe someone adept at social interaction.'
  • 豪家戚里，无不经过 - 'She had connections with all the noble families and wealthy households.' '戚里' refers to the relatives of the imperial family or the aristocracy.'
  • 追风挟策，推力渠帅 - 'She was quick to act, resourceful, and a leader among her peers.' This is a metaphorical expression praising her social influence and ability.
  • 摄衣从之，迎问曰 - 'He hurriedly put on his clothes and went to meet her, greeting her with a question.' '摄衣' is a classical expression for quickly putting on one’s clothes, often used in moments of urgency or emotion.'
  • 苏姑子作好梦也未 - 'Has Su Guzi had a good dream yet?' '苏姑子' is a colloquial term for a man, possibly a nickname or term of endearment. '作好梦' means 'had a good dream' or 'had a good fortune.'
  • 有一仙人，在下界，不邀财货，但慕风流 - 'There is a celestial being in the lower world who does not seek wealth or goods, but admires elegance and charm.' This is a poetic way of describing a man of high moral or aesthetic character.
  • 如此色目，共十郎相当矣 - 'Such beauty and character are equal to the tenth gentleman.' '色目' refers to appearance or looks, and '十郎' is a term of address for Li Yi, indicating a level of intimacy or familiarity.'
  • 引鲍手且拜且谢 - 'He took Bao’s hand and bowed and thanked her.' A formal and respectful gesture in Classical Chinese.
  • 一生作奴，死亦不惮 - 'To serve as a slave for a lifetime, even death would not be feared.' A hyperbolic expression of devotion.
  • 因问其名居 - 'He then asked for her name and residence.' A standard way of inquiring about someone’s identity.
  • 故霍王小女，字小玉 - 'She was the daughter of the late Prince Huo, named Xiao Yu.' '字' is the courtesy name, used in formal or respectful contexts.
  • 王甚爱之 - 'The prince loved her very much.' Indicates the high status and affection the father had for his daughter.
  • 母曰净持 - 'Her mother was named Jingchi.' A formal way of introducing a family member.
  • 净持，即王之宠婢也 - 'Jingchi was the prince’s favored concubine.' This explains the social status of Xiao Yu’s mother, indicating she was of lower rank but held a special place in the prince’s household.

  • 黾勉之间，强为妆梳 - Reflects the cultural expectation of women to maintain their appearance even in times of illness or distress.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence is a complex conditional structure, with the first clause serving as the basis for the subsequent clauses.
  • This phrase is a bit ambiguous, as it could be interpreted as 'with great effort, she struggled to put on makeup' or 'with great effort, she managed to put on makeup.'
  • This phrase is a bit ambiguous, as it could be interpreted as 'weak and frail in body, yet delicate and charming in appearance' or 'weak and frail in body, yet unable to maintain her charm.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '生乃召其家童，持双缣，请以备一宵之馔' is a complex action sequence where the young man summons his servant, carries silk, and offers it as a preparation for a meal.
  • The clause '宾主之仪，且不然也' sets up a contrast between the expected behavior and the actual behavior of the characters.
  • The clause '贫篓之家，随其粗粝以进之，其余以俟他辰' explains the offer of food and the promise of more on another occasion.
  • The parallel structure '帷帘榻，焕然夺目；妆奁衾枕，亦皆侈丽' describes the luxurious furnishings of the room.
  • The clause '张烛进馔，品味甚盛' describes the formal setting and the abundance of the meal.
  • The clause '彻馔，姥起' marks the end of the meal and the rising of the old woman.
  • The clause '诙谐调笑，无所不至' describes the playful interaction between the characters.
  • The long sentence '前偶过卿门，遇卿适在屏间，厥后心常勤念，虽寝与食，未尝或舍' describes the young man's memories of the woman and his constant thoughts of her.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 父子之道，天性也。使其情绝，杀而弃之，又困踬若此 - This sequence of clauses links the natural bond between father and son to the extreme actions taken, emphasizing the moral transgression.
  • 天下之人尽知为某也 - This clause highlights the public knowledge of the actions.
  • 生亲戚满朝，一旦当权者熟察其本末，祸将及矣 - This clause warns of the potential consequences if the actions are discovered by those in power.
  • 欺天负人，鬼神不，徒自遗其殃耳 - This clause summarizes the moral and spiritual consequences of the actions.
  • 姥度其志不可夺也，因许之 - This clause describes the grandmother's decision based on the son's resolve.
  • 给姥之余，有百金。离北隅四五家税一隙院，乃与生沐浴，易其衣服；为汤粥，通其肠；次以酥乳润其脏。旬余，方荐水陆之馔。头巾履袜，皆取珍异者衣之。未数月，肌肤稍腴；卒岁，平愈如初 - This long sequence describes the process of caring for the son and his gradual recovery.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 岂非秉彝之良，有不问耶 - Means 'is this not due to the inherent goodness of humanity, regardless of social status?' expressing a philosophical reflection.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 某有善婢 - I have a good maid; '某' is a humble first-person pronoun.

  • 动得人意 - Always pleases the master; '动' means 'always' and '得人意' means 'pleases the master.'
  • 请夫人听以婢安将军四体，如何？” - Please let the lady hear that the maid can ease the general's body, what do you think?; '安将军四体' means 'ease the general's body.'

  • 果然，于吾无苦耳，可促召婢来 - If it is true, it will cause no trouble for me, so please quickly summon the maid; '果然' means 'if it is true' and '于吾无苦耳' means 'it will cause no trouble for me.'

  • 帅之妻乃拥健婢数十，列白挺，炽膏镬于庭而伺之矣 - The general's wife then gathered dozens of strong maids, lined up with white spears, and heated oil in a cauldron in the courtyard, waiting for her; '拥健婢数十' means 'gathered dozens of strong maids,' '列白挺' means 'lined up with white spears,' '炽膏镬于庭' means 'heated oil in a cauldron in the courtyard,' and '伺之矣' means 'waiting for her.'
  • 须其至，当投之沸鬲 - When she arrives, she will be thrown into the boiling cauldron; '须其至' means 'when she arrives' and '投之沸鬲' means 'thrown into the boiling cauldron.'
  • 帅闻而大恐，促命止之 - The general heard and was greatly frightened, and quickly ordered to stop it; '闻而大恐' means 'heard and was greatly frightened' and '促命止之' means 'quickly ordered to stop it.'
  • 此自我意，几累于渠 - This was my own idea, almost implicating her; '自我意' means 'my own idea' and '几累于渠' means 'almost implicating her.'
  • 今幸吾之未死也，必使脱其虎喙 - Now that I am still alive, I will definitely make her escape from the tiger's mouth; '今幸吾之未死也' means 'Now that I am still alive' and '必使脱其虎喙' means 'I will definitely make her escape from the tiger's mouth.'
  • 不然，且无及矣 - Otherwise, it will be too late; '不然' means 'otherwise' and '且无及矣' means 'it will be too late.'
  • 乃大遗其奇宝，命家童榜轻舫，卫娼北归 - He then gave her a large amount of rare treasures, ordered his servants to paddle a light boat, and escorted the prostitute northward; '大遗其奇宝' means 'gave her a large amount of rare treasures,' '命家童榜轻舫' means 'ordered his servants to paddle a light boat,' and '卫娼北归' means 'escorted the prostitute northward.'
  • 自是帅之愤益振，不逾旬而物故 - From then on, the general's anger grew stronger, and he died within a few days; '自是帅之愤益振' means 'From then on, the general's anger grew stronger' and '不逾旬而物故' means 'he died within a few days.'
  • 而娼之行适及洪矣 - And the prostitute's journey just reached Hong; '适及洪矣' means 'just reached Hong.'
  • 闻至，娼乃尽返帅之赂，设位而哭曰 - When she heard, the prostitute returned all the general's gifts, set up a place, and wept, saying; '闻至' means 'When she heard,' '尽返帅之赂' means 'returned all the general's gifts,' and '设位而哭曰' means 'set up a place and wept, saying.'
  • 将军由妾而卒，将军且死，妾安用生为？ - The general died because of me, the general is about to die, why should I live?; '由妾而卒' means 'died because of me' and '妾安用生为' means 'why should I live?'
  • 妾岂孤将军者耶 - Am I the one who abandoned the general?; '岂孤将军者耶' means 'Am I the one who abandoned the general?'
  • 即撤奠而死之 - Immediately she removed the offerings and died; '撤奠' means 'removed the offerings' and '死之' means 'died.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 娼 - A prostitute, a woman who provides sexual services.

  • 洪 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 某有善婢，久给事贵室，动得人意 - This clause introduces the maid and her qualities, with '动得人意' modifying '善婢.'
  • 请夫人听以婢安将军四体，如何？” - The sentence is elliptical, with '以婢安将军四体' being the object of '听.'
  • 计未行而事泄 - The sentence is compact, with '计未行' and '事泄' forming a concessive relationship.
  • 帅之妻乃拥健婢数十，列白挺，炽膏镬于庭而伺之矣 - The sentence is complex, with '拥健婢数十' and '列白挺' forming a parallel structure, followed by '炽膏镬于庭而伺之矣.'
  • 须其至，当投之沸鬲 - The sentence is conditional, with '须其至' introducing the condition and '当投之沸鬲' the consequence.
  • 帅闻而大恐，促命止之 - The sentence is elliptical, with '促命止之' being the object of '大恐.'
  • 此自我意，几累于渠 - The sentence is concessive, with '此自我意' introducing the concession and '几累于渠' the consequence.
  • 今幸吾之未死也，必使脱其虎喙 - The sentence is conditional, with '今幸吾之未死也' introducing the condition and '必使脱其虎喙' the consequence.
  • 乃大遗其奇宝，命家童榜轻舫 - The sentence is incomplete, but it suggests that he gave her a large amount of rare treasures and ordered his servants to paddle a light boat.

  • 何期不别岁将暮 - Expresses the surprise and sadness of parting at the end of the year, a common theme in farewell poems.

  • 梦君来别如平生 - Suggests that even in dreams, the separation feels real and significant.
  • 与君已别复何别 - A rhetorical question expressing the depth of the separation and the futility of further parting.
  • 此别元乃非吉征 - Suggests that the separation is an omen of misfortune, reflecting the superstitions and beliefs of the time.
  • 万里海风掀雪浪 - Evokes the power and unpredictability of nature, symbolizing the challenges and dangers of the world.
  • 魂招不归竟长往 - Reflects the idea of a final departure, often associated with death or a permanent separation.
  • 效死君前若不知 - A pledge of ultimate loyalty, reflecting the Confucian values of filial piety and loyalty.
  • 向来宿约期无爽 - Emphasizes the importance of keeping promises, a key value in Chinese culture.
  • 二妃追舜号苍梧 - A reference to the legend of the two consorts of Emperor Shun, who followed him to the south, symbolizing loyalty and tragedy.
  • 恨染湘竹终不枯 - Refers to the legend of the Xiang River bamboo turning black due to the tears of the two consorts, symbolizing enduring sorrow.
  • 无情湘水自东注 - Describes the Xiang River flowing eastward, symbolizing the passage of time and the inevitability of change.
  • 至今斑笋盈江隅 - Refers to the spotted bamboo along the riverbank, a lasting reminder of the tragic story.
  • 屈原九歌岂不好 - A reference to Qu Yuan’s *Nine Songs*, a collection of poems, suggesting that even beautiful art can be overshadowed by deeper emotions.
  • 煎胶续弦千古无 - A metaphor for trying to mend a broken relationship, implying that some things cannot be repaired.
  • 我今试作义娼传 - The author’s statement that they are attempting to write a narrative about a courtesan, suggesting a departure from traditional themes.
  • 尚使风期后来见 - Expresses the hope that the spirit of the story will be understood by future generations.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 芳菲时节 - Poetic expression meaning 'the fragrant season' or 'the season of blooming flowers,' often used to describe springtime or the peak of floral beauty.
  • 花压枝折 - Vivid image meaning 'flowers heavy on the branches, bending them to the point of breaking,' indicating abundance.
  • 蜂蝶撩乱 - Poetic phrase meaning 'bees and butterflies in chaotic motion' or 'swarms of bees and butterflies in disarray.'
  • 栏槛光发 - Literally 'the railings and balustrades emit light,' likely a metaphor for the bright, colorful reflections of flowers on the structures.
  • 碎花魂，葬花骨 - Poetic and emotional expression, likely alluding to the death or withering of flowers, and the symbolic burial of their 'soul' and 'bones.'
  • 蜂兮蝶兮何不来 - Rhetorical question, 'Why do the bees and butterflies not come?' expressing a sense of loneliness or abandonment.
  • 空使雕栏对寒月 - 'Leaving the carved balustrade to face the cold moon alone,' a melancholic image of solitude and desolation.
  • 山作长歌答之云 - 'The mountain answers with a long song,' suggesting a poetic or mythical voice responding to the lament.
  • 东风艳艳桃李松 - 'The east wind is bright and vivid, peach and plum trees flourish,' a poetic description of springtime.
  • 龙脑透缕鲛绡红 - 'Dragon brain perfume permeates the fine red silk of a mythical fish (jiāoxiāo),' referring to luxurious and fragrant materials.
  • 鸳鸯十二罗芙蓉 - 'Twelve pairs of mandarin ducks (a symbol of love) in the pattern of lotus flowers,' likely describing ornate textiles or decoration.
  • 盈盈初见十五六 - 'She appears graceful and young, around fifteen or sixteen,' a poetic way of describing a maiden’s beauty.
  • 眉试青膏鬓垂绿 - 'She tests her eyebrows with green powder, her hair falls in green,' describing the use of cosmetics and the color of her hair.
  • 道字不正娇满怀 - 'Her pronunciation is not quite right, yet her娇态 (delicate, charming manner) fills the heart with affection.'
  • 学得襄阳大堤曲 - 'She has learned the song of the great dike of Xiangyang,' a reference to a famous love ballad or folk song.
  • 阿母偏怜掌上看 - 'Mother especially loves to watch her in her palm,' suggesting the mother's affection and the child’s delicacy.
  • 自此风流难管束 - 'From this point on, her charm is hard to restrain,' indicating the girl’s growing allure and independence.
  • 莺啄含桃未咽时 - 'When the oriole pecks at the peach, before it swallows it,' a metaphor for a moment of anticipation or desire.
  • 便念郎诗风动竹 - 'She immediately recalls her lover’s poetry, and the wind stirs the bamboo,' suggesting a romantic connection and emotional resonance.
  • 日高一丈绿窗晓 - 'The sun is a full zhang high, the green window at dawn,' describing the passage of time and the morning light.
  • 啼鸟压花新睡短 - 'The chirping birds press down on the flowers, shortening the new sleep,' a poetic way of saying the morning is bright and the flowers are fresh.
  • 腻云纤指掩还偏 - 'Her soft clouds (hair) and slender fingers cover and then slightly reveal,' a description of a woman’s delicate and coy gestures.
  • 半被可怜留翠晚 - 'Half of her is pitiable, leaving the green (hair) late,' suggesting a lingering beauty or presence.
  • 淡黄衫袖仙衣轻 - 'The light yellow sleeves of her robe are as light as fairy clothing,' describing her ethereal appearance.
  • 红玉栏杆粉妆浅 - 'The red jade railings and pale pink makeup,' a description of ornate surroundings and delicate beauty.
  • 酒痕落腮梅忍寒 - 'The wine stain falls on her cheek, the plum tree endures the cold,' a metaphor for a woman’s beauty amidst cold or hardship.
  • 春羞入目横波滟 - 'Spring’s shy gaze enters the eyes, the horizontal waves shimmer,' a poetic way of describing a woman’s gaze and beauty.
  • 一缕未消山枕红 - 'A strand of red from the mountain pillow has not yet faded,' referring to the remnants of a romantic or passionate night.
  • 斜睇整衣移步懒 - 'She glances sideways, adjusts her clothes, and moves with laziness,' a description of a woman’s drowsy, romantic demeanor.
  • 才如韩寿潘安亚 - 'Her talent is like Han Shou and Pan An, the second best,' a reference to two famous beauties or men of talent from history.
  • 掷果偷香心暗嫁 - 'She throws fruit and steals fragrance, her heart secretly betrothed,' a metaphor for a woman’s secret love or courtship.
  • 小花静院酒阑珊 - 'In the quiet courtyard with small flowers, the wine is fading,' describing a romantic, intimate setting.
  • 别有私言银烛下 - 'There are private words spoken under the silver candlelight,' a metaphor for secret conversations or romantic exchanges.
  • 帘旌浪皱金泥额 - 'The curtain waves like waves, the gold-painted forehead is wrinkled,' describing the movement of the curtain and the woman’s delicate features.
  • 六尺牙床罗帐窄 - 'The six-foot bed with silk curtain is narrow,' referring to a small, intimate sleeping space.
  • 钗横啼笑两不分 - 'The hairpin is tilted, laughter and tears are indistinguishable,' a metaphor for emotional turmoil or mixed feelings.
  • 历尽风波腰一捻 - 'Having endured waves and storms, her waist is now slender and delicate,' suggesting transformation or resilience.
  • 若教飞上九天歌，一声自可倾人国 - 'If she were to sing up to the nine heavens, one song would be enough to captivate the nation,' a hyperbolic praise of her voice or beauty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 芳菲时节 - Refers to the springtime when flowers are in full bloom, a common poetic theme in Chinese literature.
  • 花压枝折 - A vivid image from classical poetry, often used to describe the abundance of spring flowers.
  • 蜂蝶撩乱 - Bees and butterflies in disarray, a metaphor for the lively and chaotic beauty of spring.
  • 栏槛光发 - A poetic image of light reflecting off railings or balustrades, often associated with gardens or courtyards.
  • 碎花魂，葬花骨 - Likely alludes to the theme of the withering of flowers and their symbolic burial, similar to the famous '葬花吟' (The Burial of Flowers) in *Dream of the Red Chamber*.
  • 山作长歌答之云 - 'The mountain answers with a long song,' suggesting a mythic or poetic voice, possibly personifying nature or a deity.
  • 东风艳艳桃李松 - 'The east wind is bright and vivid, peach and plum trees flourish,' referencing spring and the blooming of fruit trees.
  • 龙脑透缕鲛绡红 - 'Dragon brain perfume permeates the red silk of a mythical fish,' referring to luxurious materials like dragon brain incense and silk, and the mythical *jiāoxiāo* (a type of mythical fish).
  • 鸳鸯十二罗芙蓉 - 'Twelve pairs of mandarin ducks in the pattern of lotus flowers,' likely a description of a textile or pattern.
  • 襄阳大堤曲 - A reference to a famous folk song or ballad associated with the Great Dike of Xiangyang, a historical site in Hubei.
  • 韩寿潘安 - Refers to **Han Shou** and **Pan An**, two famous figures from history known for their beauty and talent. Han Shou was a beauty in the Jin Dynasty, and Pan An (Pan Yue) was a famous poet and official known for his beauty.
  • 掷果偷香 - A metaphor for romantic or flirtatious behavior, often used in classical poetry to describe a woman’s subtle advances.
  • 银烛下 - 'Under the silver candlelight,' a common poetic setting for intimate or romantic scenes.
  • 金泥额 - 'Gold-painted forehead,' referring to a decorative forehead design or makeup.
  • 六尺牙床 - 'A six-foot bed,' a traditional Chinese bed used in literature to describe intimate settings.
  • 罗帐窄 - 'A narrow silk curtain,' often used in classical poetry to describe a small, intimate sleeping space.
  • 钗横 - 'Hairpin tilted,' a common poetic image describing a woman's disheveled or romantic state.
  • 历尽风波 - 'Having endured storms and waves,' a metaphor for enduring hardship or emotional trials.
  • 倾人国 - 'Captivate the nation,' a hyperbolic expression used to praise extraordinary beauty or talent.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 娇多必是春工与 - Poetic expression suggesting that excessive beauty or charm is often the result of the 'spring work' (春工) of nature or fate; can be interpreted as 'Excessive beauty is surely the handiwork of spring.'
  • 才能动人情几许 - Rhetorical question asking how much emotional impact or appeal can be generated by talent or beauty; can be translated as 'How much emotional sway can beauty or talent exert?'
  • 眸蹙忍羞头不举 - Vivid description of a person (likely a dancer) who is shy or hesitant, with their eyes downcast and head not lifted; can be rendered as 'Her eyes were downcast, and she hesitated, her head not raised.'
  • 凤凰萧冷曲成迟 - Metaphorical expression suggesting that the creation of a beautiful or noble work (凤凰) is slow and cold (萧冷), implying a sense of delay or difficulty; can be translated as 'The phoenix's melody was slow and cold, taking time to form.'
  • 凝醉桃花遇风雨 - Poetic image of a person (or metaphorical entity) who is intoxicated (凝醉) and encounters a storm (风雨), perhaps symbolizing a moment of vulnerability or challenge; can be rendered as 'Intoxicated with the beauty of peach blossoms, she met the storm.'
  • 阿盈阿盈听我语，劝君休向阳台住 - Direct address to someone named 阿盈, advising her to avoid the '阳台' (a place of temptation or danger); can be translated as 'Ah Ying, listen to my words; I advise you not to linger in the pleasure pavilion.'
  • 一生已有楚王怜，宋玉多才谁解赋 - Reference to historical figures: 楚王怜 (the pity of the King of Chu) and 宋玉 (a talented poet); suggests that even with the King's pity and Cao Yu's talent, true understanding is rare; can be rendered as 'Having already won the pity of the King of Chu, who among the many talents of Cao Yu could truly understand?'
  • 洛阳无限青楼女，袖掩红牙金凤缕 - Description of the many courtesans in Luoyang, with their sleeves hiding their instruments (红牙金凤缕, a type of musical instrument); can be translated as 'In Luoyang, there are countless courtesans, their sleeves concealing their red ivory and golden phoenix instruments.'
  • 春衫粉面谁家郎、只把黄金买歌舞 - Description of a young man (谁家郎) who buys entertainment (歌舞) with gold, suggesting a life of luxury and indulgence; can be rendered as 'The young man in his spring robe and powdered face, spends gold only to buy songs and dances.'
  • 就中薄幸五陵儿，一日怜新弃如土 - Reference to the '五陵儿' (young men from the Five Lings), who are fickle and discard their lovers quickly; can be translated as 'Among them, the fickle young men of the Five Lings, who in one day cherish and then cast aside like dirt.'
  • 云零雨落正堪悲，空人他人梦来去 - Poetic image of clouds and rain falling, symbolizing sorrow and the fleeting nature of dreams or relationships; can be rendered as 'The falling clouds and rain are a sight to mourn, as they come and go in the dreams of others.'
  • 浣花溪上海棠湾，薛涛朱户皆金环 - Description of the picturesque 浣花溪 (Huanhua Creek) and 海棠湾 (Haitang Bay), with the homes of 薛涛 (a famous poetess) adorned with golden rings; can be translated as 'By the Huanhua Creek, in the Haitang Bay, the homes of Xue Tao are adorned with golden rings.'
  • 韦皋笔逸玳瑁落，张祜盏滑琉璃干 - Reference to 韦皋 (a famous calligrapher) and 张祜 (a poet), suggesting their artistic brilliance; can be rendered as 'Wei Gao's brushwork is effortless, leaving behind the elegance of tortoiseshell, while Zhang Hu's wine cup is smooth as glass.'
  • 压倒念奴价百倍，兴来奇怪生毫端 - Reference to 念奴 (a famous singer), suggesting that the poet's work surpasses her in value and creativity; can be translated as 'Outshining the famous singer Nian Nu by a hundredfold, strange and wonderful ideas spring from the tip of the brush.'
  • 醉目见纸聊一扫，落花飞雪已漫漫 - Poetic image of a drunkard seeing paper and casually brushing it, creating a scene of falling flowers and snowflakes; can be rendered as 'Drunk, he sees the paper and casually brushes it, and the falling flowers and snowflakes spread endlessly.'
  • 梦得见之为改观，乐天更敢寻常看 - Reference to 梦得 (a poet) and 乐天 (another poet), suggesting that their works are so extraordinary that even they would be amazed by such art; can be translated as 'Even Meng De, upon seeing it, would change his view, and Le Tian would dare to look at it as ordinary.'
  • 花开不肯下翠幕，竟日渲赫罗雕鞍 - Poetic image of flowers refusing to lower their green curtains (翠幕), suggesting a scene of vibrant beauty and luxury; can be rendered as 'The flowers refuse to lower their green curtains, and all day long, the brilliance of the embroidered saddles shines.'
  • 扫眉涂粉至七十，老大始顶富蒲冠 - Description of an elderly woman who continues to adorn herself, even in old age, with a 蒲冠 (a hat made of reeds); can be translated as 'She sweeps her eyebrows and paints her face until seventy, and only then, in old age, does she wear the rich reed hat.'
  • 至今愁人锦江口，秋蚤露草孤坟寒 - Poignant image of the 锦江口 (Jinjiang River mouth), where a lonely grave is cold and desolate, evoking sorrow; can be rendered as 'Even now, the Jinjiang River mouth is a sorrowful place, where the lonely grave is cold and desolate.'
  • 盈盈大雅真可惜，尔生此后不可得 - Lament for the loss of a great and refined person (盈盈大雅), suggesting that such a person will not be found again; can be translated as 'Ah Ying, your great and refined beauty is truly a pity; such a person will not be found again after your time.'
  • 满天风月独倚栏，醉岸浓云呼佚墨 - Poetic image of someone standing alone by a railing, surrounded by wind, moonlight, and thick clouds, calling out to lost ink (佚墨); can be rendered as 'Under the full sky of wind and moon, she stands alone by the railing, drunk, calling out to the lost ink.'
  • 久之不见予心忆，高城去天无几尺 - Description of someone who has not been seen for a long time, evoking longing and a sense of distance; can be translated as 'After a long time, I miss you; the high city seems so close to the sky.'
  • 斜阳衔山云半红，远水无风天一碧 - Poetic image of the sun setting behind the mountains, with half-red clouds and distant, windless water, creating a scene of serene beauty; can be rendered as 'The setting sun sinks behind the mountains, the clouds half-red, and the distant water is a single shade of blue, windless and serene.'

  • 宋玉 - A famous poet and statesman of the Warring States period, known for his talent and literary works.
  • 五陵儿 - Refers to young men from the Five Lings, a region known for its wealthy and influential families during the Han dynasty, often associated with fickle behavior.
  • 薛涛 - A famous poetess of the Tang dynasty, known for her wit and literary talent.
  • 韦皋 - A famous calligrapher and politician of the Tang dynasty, known for his artistic and political achievements.

  • 念奴 - A famous singer of the Tang dynasty, known for her exceptional talent and beauty.
  • 乐天 - Refers to 白居易 (Bai Juyi), a famous poet of the Tang dynasty, known for his accessible and popular poetry.
  • 锦江口 - Refers to the mouth of the Jinjiang River in Chengdu, a historically and culturally significant location.
